skill.name: tmux
skill.description: Manage persistent tmux sessions for interactive commands, background jobs, REPLs, database shells, and log monitoring.
skill.summary: Control tmux sessions so agents can run, inspect, and clean up long-lived terminal processes.
skill.author: Bind

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution findings are mostly Markdown examples, but they reflect the skill's real purpose: running and interacting with arbitrary tmux commands. The network and weak-cryptography findings are false positives. Publish with a clear warning that this skill can execute commands and expose terminal output.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Terminal Command Execution Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill documents --command and send-command inputs that run user-provided shell commands inside tmux sessions. This is legitimate tmux functionality, but untrusted prompts could cause harmful commands to be executed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command-taking interfaces are explicitly documented and semantically intended to execute terminal commands. The risk is strong, but it is a legitimate core feature rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Terminal Output Capture May Reveal Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: The capture command returns raw terminal content from a tmux pane. Captured database prompts, logs, or REPL output may contain credentials, tokens, or private records.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly states that raw terminal content is captured and returned. Sensitive data exposure depends on what the user runs in the session.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Access Through User-Controlled Commands
medium_findings.description: Examples show log tailing, working directory selection, and local data file reads through commands run in tmux. This can access files available to the host user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples demonstrate local file and directory interaction through tmux commands. The behavior is user-directed, so the finding is moderate rather than high.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Are Benign Network References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to bun and tmux prerequisite documentation. No code in the reviewed files sends data to those URLs or performs network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in prerequisite Markdown links. I found no evidence of network exfiltration or runtime HTTP behavior in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography hits occur in ordinary documentation text, such as description, describe, descriptive, and raw output notes. No cryptographic algorithm use is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic operations or security-sensitive hashing. The scanner appears to have matched short text fragments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Terminal Command Execution Capability
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents --command and send-command inputs that run user-provided shell commands inside tmux sessions. This is legitimate tmux functionality, but untrusted prompts could cause harmful commands to be executed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command-taking interfaces are explicitly documented and semantically intended to execute terminal commands. The risk is strong, but it is a legitimate core feature rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Terminal Output Capture May Reveal Sensitive Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The capture command returns raw terminal content from a tmux pane. Captured database prompts, logs, or REPL output may contain credentials, tokens, or private records.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation clearly states that raw terminal content is captured and returned. Sensitive data exposure depends on what the user runs in the session.

content.value_statement: Interactive terminal processes often outlive a single agent response. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create, inspect, control, and clean up tmux sessions.
actual_capabilities: Lists active tmux sessions and can return structured output.
Creates named tmux sessions with an optional command, window name, and working directory.
Sends commands or special keys to a session, window, or pane.
Captures recent pane output and can wait for a matching prompt or pattern.
Terminates named sessions when work is complete.
limitations: Requires bun and tmux to be installed on the host system.
Executes commands with the permissions of the current user.
Captured output may include ANSI codes or sensitive terminal content.
The reviewed package lists command entries but does not include the referenced source files.

use_cases.title: Database Shell Management
use_cases.description: Keep a database shell open, run follow-up queries, capture results, and close the session when finished.

use_cases.title: Background Log Monitoring
use_cases.description: Start a persistent log tail, check recent output for errors, and stop monitoring after diagnosis.

use_cases.title: Interactive REPL Workflows
use_cases.description: Run a Python or Node REPL across multiple steps without losing process state between prompts.

prompt_templates.title: List Current Sessions
prompt_templates.prompt: List my active tmux sessions and tell me which ones look safe to reuse.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Log Monitor
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a tmux session named app-logs that tails my application log, then capture the latest output.

prompt_templates.title: Run Database Queries
prompt_templates.prompt: Open a tmux session for my database shell, wait for the prompt, run a count query, and show the result.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Multi-Step Debugging
prompt_templates.prompt: Create separate tmux sessions for logs and a REPL, send diagnostic commands, capture both outputs, and clean up unused sessions.

output_examples.output: A concise list of tmux session names, attached status, window counts, and which session should be reused.

output_examples.input: Capture the database session after running a query.
output_examples.output: Recent terminal output showing the prompt, the query result summary, and any visible warnings.
Confirmation that the named tmux session was terminated or a clear message if it was not found.

best_practices: Use descriptive session names that identify the project and purpose.
Review commands before sending them to sessions that can modify data or files.
Kill sessions when work is complete to avoid stale processes.
anti_patterns: Do not send commands copied from untrusted output without review.
Do not capture sessions that may display secrets unless the user requested it.
Do not leave database shells or log tails running after the task ends.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps an agent create, control, inspect, and terminate tmux sessions for long-running terminal work.

faq.question: Does it run shell commands?
faq.answer: Yes. Running commands is a core feature, so users should review commands before execution.

faq.question: Can it read terminal output?
faq.answer: Yes. It can capture raw pane output, including logs, prompts, query results, and possible secrets.

faq.question: Does it require extra software?
faq.answer: Yes. The host system needs bun and tmux installed before the documented commands can work.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because command execution is expected but powerful.

faq.question: When should sessions be closed?
faq.answer: Close sessions after the workflow finishes, especially for database shells, REPLs, and log monitors.
